subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Board
- **1 game board** — 10x10 grid with 96 card images plus 4 free corner spaces. Each card from the deck (excluding Jacks) appears exactly twice on the board.

#### Cards
- **2 standard decks of playing cards** — 104 cards total. Jacks serve as special action cards. All other cards correspond to board spaces.

#### Chips
- **50 blue chips** — For one player/team.
- **50 green chips** — For a second player/team.
- **50 red chips** — For a third player/team (3-team games).
- **35 bonus chips** — Not used in standard play.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
1. **Place the board** — Set the game board in the center of the table where all players can reach it.
2. **Note the corners** — The 4 corner spaces are free spaces that count for every player. They are already marked and do not need chips.
3. **Board grid** — The 10x10 grid shows images of playing cards. Each non-Jack card appears in exactly 2 locations on the board.
4. **No initial chips** — The board starts completely empty (aside from the printed corner free spaces).

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Team Formation
- **2 players** — Each player plays individually. Each takes one color of chips.
- **3 players** — Each player plays individually with one color each.
- **4 players** — Two teams of 2. Teammates sit across from each other.
- **6 players** — Two teams of 3, or three teams of 2. Teammates alternate seating.
- **8-12 players** — Two or three teams, divided as evenly as possible.

#### Card Deal
- Shuffle both decks together into one draw pile.
- **2 players** — Deal 7 cards each.
- **3-4 players** — Deal 6 cards each.
- **6 players** — Deal 5 cards each.
- **8-12 players** — Deal 4 cards each.

#### Chip Distribution
- Each player/team takes all chips of their assigned color.
- **2 teams** — Use blue and green.
- **3 teams** — Use blue, green, and red.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
1. **Cut for first player** — Each player cuts the deck. Lowest card goes first (Aces low).
2. **Play direction** — Turns proceed clockwise.
3. **Hand management** — Players should keep cards hidden from opponents (teammates may not share card information).
4. **No table talk** — Teammates cannot communicate about cards or strategy during play.
5. **Begin play** — The first player plays a card and places a chip, then draws a replacement card.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
1. **Play a card** — Place one card from your hand face up on the discard pile.
2. **Place or remove a chip** — Place one of your chips on a matching board space, OR use a Jack's special ability.
3. **Draw a card** — Draw one card from the draw pile to refill your hand.
4. **Announce sequences** — If you completed a sequence (5 in a row), announce it.
5. **Next player** — Play passes clockwise.

#### Important Timing Rule
- You must play your card and place your chip before drawing. If you draw first (out of order), you lose your turn and cannot place a chip.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Playing a Card
- Choose any card from your hand. Find one of the two matching spaces on the board and place your chip there.
- Since each card appears twice on the board, you choose which of the two available spaces to claim.
- If both spaces for a card are already occupied (by any color chip), the card is **dead**. You may discard it and draw a replacement on your turn, but you still forfeit placing a chip.

#### Jacks (Special Cards)
- **Two-Eyed Jacks (J with both eyes visible)** — Wild card. Place your chip on any open space on the board.
- **One-Eyed Jacks (J with one eye visible)** — Remove one opponent's chip from any board space. You cannot remove a chip that is part of a completed sequence.

#### Dead Cards
- If you hold a card whose both board positions are occupied, you may turn it in on your turn.
- Place the dead card on the discard pile, announce it is dead, and draw a new card. This uses your turn.

#### What is a Sequence?
- **5 chips in a row** — Horizontal, vertical, or diagonal.
- **Free corners count** — The 4 corner spaces act as chips for every player.
- **Shared spaces** — One chip can be part of multiple sequences for the same player/team, but only if it was part of a completed and declared sequence.
- **Locked chips** — Once a chip is part of a completed sequence, it cannot be removed by a One-Eyed Jack.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Team Communication
- **No table talk** — Teammates may not signal, hint, or discuss which cards they hold or what spaces they are targeting.
- **No card sharing** — Players cannot show their hands to teammates.
- **Observation only** — You may draw conclusions from teammates' plays but cannot ask or instruct.

#### Deck Recycling
- If the draw pile is exhausted, shuffle the discard pile to form a new draw pile.

#### Overlap Sequences
- A single chip may serve as part of two different sequences. For example, if you complete a horizontal sequence and later also form a vertical sequence sharing one chip, both count.
- However, you still need separate sequences to meet the win condition. Two sequences sharing 4 out of 5 chips do not count as 2 distinct sequences.

#### Corner Strategy
- Free corner spaces are powerful. Any sequence passing through a corner effectively needs only 4 chips instead of 5.
- Both/all teams can use the same corners.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Win Condition
- **2 players or 2 teams** — First to complete **2 sequences** wins.
- **3 players or 3 teams** — First to complete **1 sequence** wins.

#### Sequence Requirements
- Each sequence must be exactly 5 in a row (horizontal, vertical, or diagonal) of your color chips and/or free corner spaces.
- Two sequences may share at most 1 chip between them.

#### Scoring Model
- **No points** — Sequence is a race game. The first to meet the sequence requirement wins immediately.
- **No tiebreaker needed** — Only one player/team can complete the winning sequence on their turn.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
- **Target corners** — Build sequences that run through corner free spaces. This gives you a built-in head start (only 4 chips needed).
- **Claim the center** — Central board positions offer more directional options for building sequences.
- **Build two threats at once** — Place chips that contribute to multiple potential sequences. This forces opponents to choose which to block.
- **Hold your Jacks** — Two-Eyed Jacks are most valuable late in a sequence build. Don't waste them early on uncontested spaces.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
- **Tunnel vision on one line** — Opponents will block an obvious 3-in-a-row. Spread your chips to create multiple threats.
- **Wasting Two-Eyed Jacks early** — Playing a wild card when you have plenty of playable cards is wasteful. Save it for the finishing move.
- **Ignoring defense** — Letting opponents reach 4 in a row without using a One-Eyed Jack to disrupt is a losing strategy.
- **Table talk in team games** — Communicating strategy to teammates is against the rules and can result in penalty.
- **Forgetting dead cards** — Holding onto dead cards wastes turns. Check both board positions and trade in dead cards promptly.
- **Overlooking diagonal sequences** — Players tend to focus on horizontal and vertical lines but diagonals are equally valid and often less defended.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
- **Offense vs. defense** — Decide whether to extend your own sequence or block an opponent's growing line. Blocking at 3-in-a-row is usually the right call.
- **Which board space for duplicates** — Each card has 2 positions. Choose the one that best contributes to your sequence plans or disrupts an opponent's territory.
- **When to use One-Eyed Jacks** — Removing a chip from a completed sequence is impossible. Time your removal to break up opponent progress before they finish.
- **Dead card timing** — If you suspect both positions of a card are taken, check and trade in the dead card rather than holding it for multiple turns.
- **Sequence overlap planning** — Plan your second sequence to share exactly one chip with the first when possible. This is efficient and hard for opponents to predict.
